text:
Before & After

So much of what we do centers around seeing possibilities. At this 1920’s Spanish Revival house in the hills of Silver Lake, a piece of this potential was an extensive addition.

It has become faddish over the last fifty years to understand additions as opportunities for juxtaposition, for compositional dialogues – sometimes very stark in their relationship.

Our approach for this project was grounded in an understanding of the house’s lack of integrity, its lack of completeness, but also in potent remnants of its original conception – beautiful stucco walls and a period tile roof with charming bird mouthed rafter tails – key tenants of an architectural language that is synonymous with Los Angeles.

Sadly, the 1200 sq/ft house had been quickly renovated by the last owner – new vinyl windows, big box store tile, the works – most of the original details lost. This house, the artifact, lacked the force and quality to command an architectural dialogue. Instead, the new 1500 sq/ft wing was seen as a grafting on to the old – an extension of it, completing the original idea in our time, while satisfying the needs of the present: a large kitchen – a place to gather and to entertain, proper service spaces and storage, a principal suite, and closet after closet. 

Photographs by Virtually Here Studios

#spanishrevival #oldlosangeles #breland-harper
A practice in restraint. The Modernist Villa by Breland-Harper, Los Angeles.
The Californians - @Inglenook, Francis Ford Coppola and Eleanor Coppola. Designed by Hamden McIntyre, 1887.

I didn’t plan on posting about this, but it’s been sitting with me for quite a while now. 

There’s always been a bit of tension in my mind between my for-profit work and the nonprofit my wife @mama_zim and I started, @sevenhousesorg, after the LA fires. The truth is, it’s not separate…it’s the same work. It’s all about people, and doing what we can to show up when it matters.

Los Angeles has given us a lot. Loving this city and supporting the people in it is just part of how we build.

Honored to be included in @archdigest LA issue and grateful to be in the room with so many people pushing design forward in meaningful ways. Thank you to the AD team @amyastley @samuelcochran for the recognition and the incredible event.

And to Peter and Michael at @breland_harper ….thank you for giving your time and talent to help others rebuild and most importantly, thank you for your friendship. 

Lastly, my daughter and style queen for the night @zozogal, thanks for flying out to party!!! ❤️

A look back at one of our favorite projects and recipient of the AIA Honor Award — our mid-century pavilion in Pasadena’s San Rafael Heights.

We reimagined the home as a treehouse oasis, staying true to the neighborhood’s legacy of finely crafted mid-century modernism and a design tradition shaped by the West Coast’s climate and cultural hybridity.

Vacant and derelict for fourteen years, the house still revealed its original intent: a simple pavilion on pilotis, suspended within a serene garden and mature canopy. Its deep eaves and post-and-beam structure echo the language of postwar contemporaries like Buff & Hensman and Vladimir Ossipoff — architects who fused Japanese aesthetics, European modernism, and Arts and Crafts into a distinctly Californian vernacular.

The renovation is a contemporary homage to that lineage, defined by strong indoor-outdoor connections and a careful orchestration of light, air, and shadow.

This is a home designed to settle gracefully into its longevity, extending its Southern California context for years to come.

A new wave of spas and salons are turning to top interior designers to create spaces as considered as the finest homes. At @maisonlaperche in Studio City, designers Michael Breland and Peter Harper of @breland_harper channeled the elegance of 1920s and '30s Parisian beauty salons with Art Deco club chairs in pewter velvet, a mahogany Art Moderne credenza, and vintage Frits Henningsen furniture. Every treatment room gets "one great piece," and swirling Fortuny lamps overhead ensure that even 90 minutes of staring at the ceiling feels wonderful. For our April 2026 issue, writer @sbrogue takes us inside. Tap the link in our bio to read.

Photos by @austinleis of @maisonlaperche by @breland_harper

A great night with great company, celebrating @sevenhousesorg’s feature in the @archdigest March issue. Visit their website to learn more about their mission to preserve and protect the architectural legacy of Los Angeles.

A breakfast room in Malibu — it’s all really about the view.

The Californians - Casa del Herrero, George Fox Steedman and Carrie Robb Howard. Designed by George Washington Smith, 1925.
Where it all begins – a site visit to a ground-up house currently in construction in the Palisades.

Santa Fe, somewhere we return time and time again – its rich textures, exquisite colors, vast deserts, and storied architecture are endlessly inspiring. 

Here, snapshots from a trip this winter, fragments of artistry and detail.
